item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
just realized that basil ottinger pops up a few times around the wiki; should he have his own tag?

+1, by the way, loved the pacing AND the special effects in this one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looked up a few more paintings by Hammershøi after reading this. Love how this skip's description and backstory have a similar quality to his art: subdued, seemingly normal, but with a strange tension and mystery about it all.

+1 for sure

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops; thanks for the catch!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The link for Picture C-1 appears to be wrong. It once again links to art1.png just like Picture A-1 instead of, I assume, art3.png.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that the article lacking all five pieces makes it feel like an incomplete work, but that's a small mistake compared to the overall quality of the article.

Thanks! I should mention that failing to provide all the pieces (there are six, by the way!) was a conscious decision on my part. I actually have Hammershøi pieces that would work for each painting, but I realized early on that I wanted the paintings to be part of the article (revealing them to the reader at specific points, culminating in the final reveal). Since the article was going to be short, I also knew I had limited space to do this with (enough for 3 pictures, and just barely).

On top of all that, this article was an exercise in restraint for me. I realized that I didn't want the reader to see the five figures praying at the kitchen table, for example — I wanted the reader to imagine it.

(Also, yeah, fine; I'll admit it: I'm lazy and didn't want to edit this painting to have a fifth figure, or make all the other figures dark and praying)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this one. It reminds me of Ib, in a way. The pictures are also pretty nice. I think that the article lacking all five pieces makes it feel like an incomplete work, but that's a small mistake compared to the overall quality of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an aside, is the hand sometimes seen in the Foyer painting supposed to belong to Marie Holdstock?

That was my intent, yes — though I wanted to try and leave open the possibility of something else. But given the juxtaposition of the last image with the last sentence of the article, this is definitely what the text 'wants' you to conclude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, I understand its a slightly intractable problem. As an aside, is the hand sometimes seen in the Foyer painting supposed to belong to Marie Holdstock?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is that the had to do it to em guy in the first image?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough — although keep in mind, they weren't in the cellar the whole time:

Despite multiple reports from neighbors who claimed to have witnessed members of his family standing at the windows

But yeah, I couldn't figure out any way to pack in the implication they were corpses without actually finding the corpses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is neat but the revelation that the family were dead in the cellar the whole time is honestly a bit much, and detracts from the low-key creepiness of the rest of the piece. I really like the implication that the 'silhouettes' were corpses rather than ghosts, but him turning his family into mannequins is both really campy and a little stale as SCP plot twists go.

item.title: Thanks
item.content:encoded:
That's awesome, I all ways love when the pics transform
+1
PS I got a rainbow scp logo…awesome!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like to think he isn't the one who killed them; he just posed them afterwards.

Admittedly, the text doesn't imply this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ohhh baby this hit the spot in so many right ways. +1 without hesitation. though you have to admit the old crazy guy killing whole family trope can be freshened up a bit at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. It has a somewhat somber feel to it without going on for too long. The images are also pretty cool.

+1

Also, I’m glad to see my coding tricks used here, even if it was just the code from SCP-2808 that I jury-rigged to be more biased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It appears that the images actually change. The last one might have scared me when it did.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely aside of the contents of this article (which I like, for the record), I'm seriously digging that little info tab next to the rating module and was slightly disappointed to learn that it wasn't a new sitewide feature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chilling, just like much of your other stuff. Definitely an upvote from me :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops; thanks — I linked the image that I uploaded rather than the source. Fixed — I appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Link for "artc.png" source seems to be wrong. The exact one is here, it's public domain.

I'm so fascinated by the disquieting background and remaining mysteries of this article. Great work as always!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Updated the skip with code stolen from not_a_seagull's SCP-4948 (thanks for the suggestion to those in chat, and people in discord for talking me through how to implement it!).

Images now change (each has one variant). Check the 'Spoilers' collapsible in the post above for how to access them without having to hit refresh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought I’d sensed a Hinterkaifeck vibe from this one. Excellent work Hippo, and very haunting. I love how the hand at the window looks real rather than painted, accentuating the disturbing nature of the scene.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew what was going to happen as soon as I hit the part about the family going missing, but as in most of your articles, it's more about the journey than the destination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your minimalism soars here, an excellent and memorable read. The longer I looked at the images, the more unsettled I got.

Edit: Nice song choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a delightfully foreboding mystery- you do a great job of building up the atmosphere here. Clear +1.

Trying to work out scenarios of precisely what happened is going to occupy my mind for some time :p.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! And yeah, 'general store' is probably what I should have used (fixing that now).

ETA: Also, minmin introduced me to Hammershøi's stuff, and as soon as I saw it I knew I had to do something like this with it (it's just so muted and foreboding).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was at an art gallery yesterday and saw paintings by Hammershøi for the first time, so this is very surreal to see.

There seem to be an awful lot of Hinterkaifeck SCPs now, but I don't notice anything substantive to criticise about the writing, and the images bring this together very nicely. +1

The use of the term 'convenience store' to refer to a store in 1900s Germany sounds off to me, though I suppose it could be the correct term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And either way you turn
I'll be there
Open up your skull
I'll be there

Climbing up the walls
Climbing up the walls
Climbing up the walls

SOURCES:

  • The paintings are by Vilhelm Hammershøi (a Danish painter known for his low-key style), edited by me; each is CC compliant. Links here, here, here, here, and here.
  • I've modified the last painting using the image from here.
  • Inspired by the Hinterkaifeck murders and The Reaper's Image (a short story by Stephen King).
  • Code stolen from not_a_seagull's SCP-4948.
SPOILERS!
– hide block

Click refresh a couple of times. The pictures change.

If you don't want to wait, here's links to each picture and its variant:

  • Picture A-1 / Picture A-2
  • Picture B-1 / Picture B-2
  • Picture C-1 / Picture C-2

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
